title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: User-supplied query parameter is directly concatenated into a raw SQL query string without parameterization. An attacker can craft input such as `%' UNION SELECT ...--` to extract arbitrary data from the database. This can expose all user credentials and sensitive records.

title: SQL Injection in Login Route
description: Email and password fields are directly interpolated into a raw SQL query, enabling authentication bypass via classic payloads like `' OR 1=1--`. An attacker can log in as any user, including administrators, without knowing the password. This completely defeats authentication.

title: Remote Code Execution via eval() in User Profile
description: User-supplied username matching the pattern `#{...}` is passed directly to `eval()`, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution on the server. An attacker who sets their username to `#{require('child_process').exec('...')}` achieves full RCE. This is the highest-severity class of vulnerability.

title: Unsafe eval() in CAPTCHA Generation
description: A mathematical expression is evaluated via `eval()` without sanitization. If the expression can be influenced by attacker-controlled input upstream, this becomes a code injection vector. Even if currently server-side-only, use of `eval()` on untrusted data is inherently dangerous.

title: NoSQL Injection via $where Operator
description: The MongoDB query uses the `$where` operator with a string interpolation of the user-supplied product ID, allowing JavaScript injection into the database engine. An attacker can craft a payload like `0;return true` to match unintended documents. MongoDB `$where` executes JavaScript on the server and must never include user input.

title: XXE via XML Upload with noent:true
description: XML files are parsed with `libxmljs.parseXml()` using the option `noent: true`, which enables entity expansion and external entity processing. An attacker can upload a crafted XML file referencing external entities to read server-side files (e.g. `/etc/passwd`) or trigger SSRF. Disabling `noent` or using a safe XML parser is required.

title: Path Traversal in Zip File Extraction
description: Zip entry filenames are not properly canonicalized before being used to construct file paths, allowing a Zip Slip attack. An attacker can include entries with paths like `../../app.js` to overwrite arbitrary files outside the upload directory. The `path.resolve` check is insufficient because it tests containment rather than prefix.

title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
description: Passwords are hashed with MD5, a fast, cryptographically broken algorithm with no salting. MD5 password hashes can be cracked in seconds using rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated dictionary attacks. Passwords must be hashed with bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 with an appropriate work factor.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key in Source Code
description: An RSA private key is embedded directly in source code, meaning anyone with read access to the repository can forge JWT tokens signed with this key. This fully breaks token-based authentication since all signed tokens can be replicated. Private keys must be loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager at runtime.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret
description: An HMAC secret `'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj'` is hardcoded in source code and committed to version control. Any attacker who views the source can compute valid HMACs for arbitrary messages, breaking message integrity guarantees. Secrets must be injected at runtime from environment variables or a vault.

title: Hardcoded CTF Key in Source Control
description: A plaintext secret key `TRwzkRJnHOTckssAeyJbysWgP!Qc2T` is committed to the repository. Secrets committed to version control are effectively public once the repo is cloned or its history is inspected. This key should be rotated and stored as a runtime secret.

title: JWT Algorithm Confusion / None Algorithm Accepted
description: JWT verification accepts tokens using the `none` algorithm, which requires no signature at all. An attacker can craft an unsigned token with any payload (including admin roles) and the server will accept it as valid. JWT libraries must be configured to only accept explicitly allowlisted algorithms.

title: Directory Listing Enabled for /ftp and /support/logs
description: The `serveIndex` middleware is enabled for the `/ftp` and `/support/logs` endpoints, allowing any unauthenticated user to browse and download all files in those directories. This exposes backup files, log files, and potentially sensitive documents. Directory listing should be disabled in production.

title: Encryption Keys Directory Publicly Browsable
description: The `/encryptionkeys` directory is served with directory listing enabled, making any stored encryption keys discoverable and downloadable by unauthenticated users. Exposure of encryption keys allows decryption of all data protected by those keys. This endpoint must be removed or require strong authentication.

title: BOLA: Basket Access Without Ownership Verification
description: The basket ID validation uses a loose numeric comparison and has an early-exit bypass when `basketIds[0]` is `'undefined'`. An attacker can manipulate basket IDs in requests to read or modify other users' shopping carts. Ownership checks must use strict equality and be enforced server-side on every request.

title: BOLA: Payment Methods Accessed via User-Controlled UserId
description: The payment card lookup queries the database using a `UserId` value taken directly from the request body, which the client fully controls. An attacker can set `UserId` to another user's ID to enumerate, view, or delete their saved payment methods. The server must derive `UserId` from the authenticated session token, never from the request body.

title: Broken IP-Based Access Control (Invalid CIDR Range)
description: The accounting API endpoint uses `IpFilter` with an invalid IP address `'123.456.789'`, which can never be a valid IP and will never match. Depending on the library's failure mode (allow-all or deny-all on invalid config), this either removes the access control entirely or permanently blocks legitimate access. The filter must use a valid IP/CIDR range.

title: Stored XSS in Product Reviews
description: User-submitted review messages are stored in MongoDB and later rendered without HTML sanitization. An attacker can store a script payload in a review that executes in the browsers of all users who view that product. Stored XSS has wide blast radius since it persists and affects every visitor.

title: DOM XSS via Username in Pug Template with unsafe-eval CSP
description: User-controlled username is substituted directly into a Pug template string and the Content-Security-Policy header includes `'unsafe-eval'`, negating script-src restrictions. Combined with the eval()-based code injection, an attacker can achieve persistent XSS through a crafted username. The CSP should not include `'unsafe-eval'` and template interpolation must be escaped.

title: Unsafe YAML Deserialization
description: Uploaded YAML files are parsed with `yaml.load()` (js-yaml v3) without specifying a safe schema, allowing instantiation of arbitrary JavaScript types. Malicious YAML files can trigger gadget chains or DoS via a YAML bomb. The safe alternative is `yaml.safeLoad()` or passing `{ schema: yaml.FAILSAFE_SCHEMA }` in newer versions.

title: Wildcard CORS Policy Allows All Origins
description: The application explicitly enables CORS for all origins with `app.use(cors())` and no origin restriction, as noted in a comment: "Bludgeon solution for possible CORS problems: Allow everything!". This allows any website to make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API. CORS should be restricted to a specific allowlist of trusted origins.

title: X-XSS-Protection / Helmet XSS Filter Explicitly Disabled
description: The Helmet XSS filter middleware is commented out with the note that it provides no protection from persisted XSS via the REST API. While modern browsers have deprecated this header, disabling it removes a defense-in-depth layer for older browsers. Additionally, no Content-Security-Policy is configured to compensate.

title: Missing Security Headers (Incomplete Helmet Configuration)
description: Only `noSniff` and `frameguard` Helmet middlewares are enabled; HSTS, CSP, referrer-policy, and permissions-policy headers are absent. Missing security headers increase attack surface across XSS, clickjacking, and protocol downgrade vectors. A full Helmet configuration should be applied.

title: Open Redirect via Substring URL Validation
description: The redirect allowlist check uses `url.includes(allowedUrl)` (substring match) instead of an exact origin match or `url.startsWith()`. An attacker can bypass the allowlist with a URL like `https://evil.com/redirect?to=https://github.com/juice-shop` which contains an allowed string. This enables phishing attacks using the application as an open redirector.

title: Rate Limit Bypass via X-Forwarded-For Spoofing
description: The password reset rate limiter uses `headers['X-Forwarded-For']` as the rate-limit key when present, and `app.enable('trust proxy')` is set globally. An attacker can bypass the rate limit entirely by cycling arbitrary `X-Forwarded-For` header values across requests. Rate limiting keys must be derived from a trusted network-level source, not a user-controlled header.

title: No CSRF Tokens on State-Changing Endpoints
description: Profile update and other state-changing endpoints lack CSRF token validation; the code only detects CSRF originating from a specific external domain for a challenge. Without CSRF tokens, any malicious third-party site can submit authenticated requests on behalf of logged-in users. CSRF protection must cover all state-changing operations.

title: jsonwebtoken v0.4.0 (2013, EOL) with Critical CVEs
description: The application depends on `jsonwebtoken` version `0.4.0`, released in 2013 and many major versions behind the current 9.x release. This ancient version contains multiple critical vulnerabilities including algorithm confusion attacks and key confusion that allow token forgery. The dependency must be upgraded to the latest stable release.

title: express-jwt v0.1.3 (Ancient, Multiple CVEs)
description: The `express-jwt` middleware is pinned to version `0.1.3`, an extremely outdated release with known critical vulnerabilities in JWT signature verification. Attackers can exploit these to bypass authentication middleware entirely. This package must be upgraded or replaced with a maintained alternative.

title: sanitize-html v1.4.2 with XSS Bypass Vulnerabilities
description: The `sanitize-html` package is pinned to version `1.4.2`, which has known XSS bypass vulnerabilities that allow crafted inputs to pass through the sanitizer and execute as scripts. Any content sanitized with this version cannot be trusted as safe for rendering. The package must be upgraded to a current release.

title: js-yaml v3.x with Unsafe Load Vulnerabilities
description: The `js-yaml` package is constrained to the `^3.14.0` range; v3's `yaml.load()` allows arbitrary object construction which can be exploited for deserialization attacks. The safe loader was only made the default in v4+. Combined with `yaml.load()` usage in the file upload route, this creates a concrete deserialization risk.

title: libxmljs2 v0.37.x with XXE Vulnerabilities
description: The `libxmljs2` dependency is pinned to `~0.37.0`, an outdated version with known XXE and memory safety vulnerabilities. Combined with the `noent: true` parsing option used in the upload route, this creates a directly exploitable XXE attack surface. The library must be updated and XXE protections enabled.

title: Sensitive Log Files Publicly Accessible
description: Application logs are served from `/support/logs` with directory listing enabled and no authentication required. Server logs frequently contain session tokens, internal error stack traces, internal IP addresses, and user PII. These files must be removed from the public web root or protected behind authentication.
